questions.question: Explain the concept of inverse distance weighting in weighted KNN.
expectedPoints: Definition of inverse distance weighting
Mathematical formula: w = 1/d
Purpose: closer neighbors get higher weights
Advantages over equal weighting

questions.question: Compare the storage requirements and prediction time complexity of weighted KNN and nearest centroid classifier.
expectedPoints: Weighted KNN stores all training data: O(nd) space
Nearest centroid stores only centroids: O(cd) space
Weighted KNN prediction: O(nd + K) time
Nearest centroid prediction: O(cd) time
Explanation of why these differences exist

questions.question: Describe the steps involved in the nearest centroid classification algorithm.
expectedPoints: Training phase: calculate centroid for each class
Centroid calculation: mean of feature vectors
Prediction phase: compute distances to centroids
Assignment: class of nearest centroid
Example with simple calculation

questions.question: Discuss the advantages and limitations of weighted KNN compared to standard KNN.
expectedPoints: Advantage: more accurate when closer neighbors are more relevant
Advantage: reduced influence of outliers
Advantage: smoother decision boundaries
Limitation: more computationally intensive
Limitation: requires choice of weighting function
Limitation: still suffers from curse of dimensionality
Practical considerations for implementation

questions.question: Explain why feature scaling is crucial for distance-based algorithms like weighted KNN and nearest centroid, and describe two common scaling methods.
expectedPoints: Problem: features with larger ranges dominate distance
Solution: scaling ensures equal feature contribution
Min-max scaling: formula and explanation
Z-score normalization: formula and explanation
Impact on algorithm performance
Practical implementation considerations

questions.question: Analyze scenarios where nearest centroid classifier would be preferred over KNN variants and vice versa, considering factors like dataset size, computational resources, and data distribution.
expectedPoints: When to use nearest centroid: large datasets, limited memory, fast prediction needed
When to use nearest centroid: spherical class distributions
When to use KNN: complex decision boundaries, multimodal classes
Computational trade-offs: storage vs prediction time
Real-world examples for each scenario
Hybrid approaches consideration
